answer:
Cynewulf embeds his name as a runic acrostic signature at the close of *Elene* and *Juliana* (as well as *Andreas*), a rare explicit self-assertion of authorship in Old English verse.[^1][^2] These signatures appear as a sequence of runes (ᚳᚣᚾᛖᚹᚢᛚᚠ, transliterated as *C Y N E W U L F*) formed by the initial letters of words in a personal, penitential passage. In *Elene*, Cynewulf reflects on his sinful life, weaving his name amid pleas for salvation: "A was secg oth that cnyssed cearwelmum, / C drusende... Y gnornode / N gefera... enge rune... E fore... W is geswithrad... U was geara... L toglideth... F aghwam bith."[^3] Similarly, *Juliana* ends: "Yeomor hweorfeth C Y ond N, / Chyning bith rethe... synnum fah E W ond U / acle bidath... L F beofath."[^4] This pattern personalizes the poems' conclusions, shifting from hagiographic narrative to the poet's *I*-voiced confession of sin ("Ic was weorcum fah, synnum asaled"), hope for divine mercy, and craft ("leothucraft onleac"). It asserts authorial presence as a flawed individual seeking eternal reward, blending humility with bold inscription.

In contrast, the Old English elegies in the corpus (*The Wanderer*, *The Seafarer*, *Widsith*, etc.) maintain near-total anonymity, typical of the genre.[^5] No named poet emerges; "authorial presence" manifests indirectly through dramatic *I*-speakers who embody archetypal exile (*anhaga*), loss, or wandering (*wraecwthmood* in *The Wanderer*, ch. 11). These voices evoke collective Anglo-Saxon anxieties—transience (*eorla eal gesweacað*, "all the might of earls decays")—without personal signatures or biographical intrusion.[^6] The effect is impersonal universality: the speaker is *everyman*, not a historical "Cynewulf." Refrains like "Thus spoke the wanderer..." (ch. 11) frame the voice as exemplary, not proprietary.[^7]

Cynewulf's runes thus innovate against this anonymous norm, "relating" as a deliberate rupture. Elegies prioritize communal lament (e.g., *Maxims I*, ch. 6: gnomic wisdom without ego); Cynewulf claims the text as his artifact (*wordcraftum waf*, "wove with wordcraft"), akin to a sculptor's mark. This elevates authorship from submerged tradition to visible individuality, perhaps compensating for oral anonymity or seeking prayers for his soul. Yet both modes share Christian penitence: elegies' speakers yearn for divine consolation (*heofona healm*, "heaven's protection"); Cynewulf's signature explicitly ties craft to salvation. The elegies' "closeness to anonymity" underscores Cynewulf's exceptionality in a corpus otherwise favoring veiled presence.[^8]

[^1]: *Cynewulf - Elene*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [full chapter](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-624562).
[^2]: *Cynewulf - Juliana*, ch. 1 (transliteration), [full chapter](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/translation/#p-01394b).
[^3]: *Elene*, ch. 1 [passage](/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/transliteration/#p-624562) (final ~100 lines).
[^4]: *Juliana*, ch. 1 [passage](/works/cynewulf-juliana-9a2157/01/transliteration/#p-01394b) (final ~20 lines).
[^5]: *Old English Elegies* (17 chapters); no authorial names in wiki or texts. See ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), [full](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11/translation/).
[^6]: *The Wanderer*, ch. 11 [passage](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11/translation/#p-992aeb) ("Where has the horse gone? Where the young man?... All earth's kingdom is dreary").
[^7]: Ibid., refrain structure noted in wiki card.
[^8]: Corpus wiki cards flag no signatures in elegies; Cynewulf's are statistically distinctive (e.g., "enge rune" unique trigram in *Elene*).
